claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that e-commerce businesses are growth-constrained not by execution but by capital availability, supply chain throughput, and distribution capacity; these are structural features of the model, not operational failures. A business can have zero execution problems and still be blocked from growing if it has sold its inventory before it can afford to reorder.
conditions: e-commerce or physical product business
growing faster than free cash flow allows reinvestment
claims.mechanism: Physical inventory must be purchased before it can be sold; profit from earlier sales must fund next inventory cycle, so the growth rate is capped by the free cash flow percentage the business generates, not by demand or marketing skill.
bounded_by: Does not apply to dropship models where inventory is never held
Does not apply to flat e-commerce businesses that are not trying to grow

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that service businesses are almost always supply-constrained rather than demand-constrained because good people are harder to find and replicate than good products, and finding additional skilled labor becomes exponentially harder at higher skill tiers.
conditions: done-for-you service business
quality of delivery is tied to individual skill level
claims.mechanism: A service business sells the labor of skilled people at a premium; demand for the business can grow faster than the rate at which skilled labor can be trained or recruited, creating a permanent supply ceiling.
bounded_by: Fitness coaching businesses are unique in that talent is easy to attract because people would do it for free, so their constraint is demand and retention
Low-skill service businesses with large labor pools, such as cleaning, face demand constraints in some markets

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that in education businesses, there is an inherent tension between scaling delivery and maintaining quality: diluting access to the best teacher by training less-skilled instructors is analogous to adding water to premium milk. Most operators who try to scale delivery through trained instructors end up damaging their reputation because the instructor quality cannot match the founder's skill level.
conditions: core value proposition is founder's expert knowledge
education business attempting to scale via trained instructors
claims.mechanism: The founder's skill is the primary value; instructors trained in a weekend carry only a fraction of that value; clients who pay premium prices for the founder implicitly expect founder-level quality and receive diluted quality, which reduces word-of-mouth and damages reputation.
bounded_by: Founders who continue to teach one-to-many without scaling through instructors can maintain quality while slowly building brand; technology enables one teacher to reach unlimited students without quality dilution

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that SaaS businesses have higher gross margins and greater enterprise value potential than the other three business models once product-market fit is achieved, but they require more upfront capital and time before generating meaningful revenue. The primary reason for this structural disadvantage early on is that customers' willingness to pay for software is very low because they perceive the incremental cost as near zero.
conditions: SaaS or software business at early stage, pre-product-market fit
claims.mechanism: Customers know software has near-zero marginal cost to deliver, so they anchor willingness to pay very low (e.g., $9-$13/month for consumer SaaS); this requires enormous volume to generate real profit, which requires more capital and time than the other three models.
bounded_by: Enterprise SaaS can command higher prices relative to consumer SaaS because switching costs are higher and budget access is larger, but the fundamental capital-intensity of the early period remains

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that in SaaS, virality is required at scale because the willingness to pay per user is so low that paid acquisition alone cannot sustainably drive growth. A viral coefficient greater than 1 means each acquired user generates more than one additional user, compounding down the effective cost of acquisition over time.
conditions: consumer or SMB SaaS where price per user is under ~$100/month
claims.mechanism: Low unit revenue means breakeven CAC is low; viral loops effectively multiply each paid acquisition by the viral coefficient, reducing blended CAC as the product scales without proportional increase in paid acquisition spend.
bounded_by: Enterprise SaaS with high ACV ($50k+/year) can grow through direct sales and paid acquisition without requiring virality; virality is most critical for low-ACV products
